message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://vuink.com/post/fghss-d-dvagreserr-d-dpn/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders-d-dhtml
**Title:** The State of Modern AI Text-To-Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (2026 practitioner write-up)
**Found via:** engines-config + measurement search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
# Analysis of Web Page Content

**Publication Date:** 2026-01-23

**Author:** Style Pass (submitted to Vuink.com aggregator)

**Source Type:** Personal blog/practitioner write-up (appears to be a repost from stuff.interf...)

---

## Relevant Claims Extracted

### High-Speed Listening Preferences
The article states that blind screen reader users prefer voices optimized differently than sighted audiences: "blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient."

### Speaking Rate Specifications
Direct measurement provided: users favor listening at "often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute" compared to the baseline "average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute."

### Intelligibility-Naturalness Tradeoff
The piece explicitly identifies this tension: "While sighted users prefer voices that are natural, conversational, and as human-like as possible, blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic."

### Eloquence Voice (Formant Synthesizer)
Key claims about this legacy engine: "the voice that is preferred by the majority of western English blind users, called Eloquence, was last updated in 2003" and it is "a 32-bit voice last compiled in 2003, [that] cannot run in modern software without some sort of emulation or bridge."

### Technical Barriers
The article notes that "even Apple...were forced to use an emulation layer" and discusses "known security issues" preventing updates.

**No measurements comparing neural vs. formant TTS performance, or mentions of Kokoro, Piper, Azure, Google, or ElevenLabs appear in this excerpt.**

content.text: Let me fetch additional content to check for more specific claims about neural TTS engines and latency, since the first fetch noted some engines weren't covered in the excerpt.

Quote exact text. Also confirm the original author name and original publication source if stated.

input.prompt: Extract all claims relevant to: TTS/voice intelligibility at high-speed listening; screen-reader power users' preferences between formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) vs neural/AI TTS at high speeds (e.g. 800-900 words per minute); naturalness-vs-intelligibility tradeoff; latency/responsiveness/consistency/predictability of voices; speaking-rate control; specific neural engines (Kokoro, Piper, Azure, Google, ElevenLabs, RHVoice); any measurements or quantified comparisons. Report the publish date and author. Quote the exact text for any claim you extract.

content.content:
# TTS Claims Extraction

**Author:** Samuel Proulx  
**Published:** January 5, 2026

## High-Speed Listening Preferences

"blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient...understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute."

## Formant Synthesizer vs. Neural TTS

The author expresses strong preference for legacy synthesizers:
- **Eloquence:** "last updated in 2003" and "overwhelmingly popular" among Western English blind users
- **eSpeak-ng:** acknowledged as meeting blind user needs but based on 1995 architecture with limited maintenance
- Neural systems (Kitten TTS, Supertonic, Kokoro): all deemed "unsuitable for use in screen readers"

## Naturalness vs. Intelligibility Tradeoff

"Modern text to speech research...prefer voices that are natural, conversational...human-like" contrasts with blind user requirements. The author notes: "these modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy."

## Critical Issues with Neural TTS

**Accuracy:** Both Kitten TTS and Supertonic "had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints."

**Latency:** "cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text" versus older systems enabling rapid interruption.

**Voice Control Loss:** "Changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize" is "easy" with legacy systems but impossible with trained neural models.

**Dependency Bloat:** Kitten TTS requires "around 103" Python packages; Supertonic needs "just over 30."

## Specific Engines Mentioned

Only Kitten TTS, Supertonic, and Kokoro are explicitly discussed. No quantified performance comparisons provided between different neural engines.

content.text: I now have the full content from the original source. Let me verify a couple of the more specific quantified claims by fetching targeted details.

input.prompt: Quote the exact sentences about: (1) the latency problem where neural TTS cannot begin generating speech until it has a whole chunk of text, and why interruption/responsiveness matters for screen reader users; (2) the inability to change pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, head size on neural voices versus legacy synthesizers; (3) the dependency/package count for Kitten TTS and Supertonic; (4) the exact words-per-minute figures. Give verbatim quotes only.

content.content:
# Quotes from "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems"

**(1) Latency and interruption:**
"Unlike older text to speech systems, Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text."

"Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech."

**(2) Voice customization limitations:**
"Older text to speech systems make changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters of the voice easy."

"AI text to speech models, being trained on data from a particular set of speakers, cannot offer this customization."

**(3) Dependency counts:**
"In the case of Kitten TTS, the number is around 103, and just over 30 for supertonic."

**(4) Words-per-minute figures:**
"blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient...often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute."

"The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison."

content.text: I have all the verified content I need. This is a personal practitioner blog by Samuel Proulx, highly relevant to angles (2), (3), and (5) of the research question.

claims.claim: Blind screen-reader power users prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient, listening at 800-900 words per minute versus the 200-250 wpm of an average speaker, accepting a robotic sound as a trade-off for accuracy.
claims.quote: blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient...understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. ... The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison.

claims.claim: Modern neural TTS systems are deemed unsuitable for screen readers because their human-natural design comes at the expense of accuracy, with engines like Kitten TTS and Supertonic tending to skip words, misread numbers, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints.
claims.quote: these modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy. ... had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints.

claims.claim: Neural TTS introduces a latency/responsiveness problem fatal for screen-reader use: it cannot begin generating speech until it has an entire chunk of text, whereas screen-reader users frequently interrupt and need the engine to instantly discard and restart speech.
claims.quote: Unlike older text to speech systems, Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text. ... Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech.

claims.claim: Legacy/formant synthesizers allow easy real-time control of pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, and head size, while neural models trained on fixed speaker data cannot offer this customization.
claims.quote: Older text to speech systems make changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters of the voice easy. ... AI text to speech models, being trained on data from a particular set of speakers, cannot offer this customization.

claims.claim: Eloquence, the synthesizer overwhelmingly preferred by Western English blind users, was last updated in 2003 and is a 32-bit binary that cannot run in modern software without an emulation layer, illustrating why no modern neural voice has displaced legacy formant synthesis.
claims.quote: the voice that is preferred by the majority of western English blind users, called Eloquence, was last updated in 2003 ... a 32-bit voice last compiled in 2003, [that] cannot run in modern software without some sort of emulation or bridge.

content.text:
I've completed the source extraction. 

The vuink.com URL was an aggregator excerpt that linked to the original article at `https://stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html`, which I fetched and verified directly.

Summary of findings:
- **Source**: "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users" by Samuel Proulx, published 2026-01-05. This is a personal practitioner blog (classified as `blog` quality) from someone in the blind/screen-reader community, not peer-reviewed research.
- **Relevance**: Strongly relevant to research angles (2) formant vs. neural at extreme speed, (3) rate/prosody control, and (5) engine comparison. It provides the practitioner/community perspective the research question explicitly asked for.

Five falsifiable claims extracted and returned via StructuredOutput, the three central ones being: (1) the 800-900 wpm preference with robotic-sound trade-off, (2) neural TTS unsuitability due to accuracy failures (word-skipping, number misreads, prosody-ignoring), and (3) the streaming-latency/interruptibility barrier. Two supporting claims cover loss of real-time voice parameter control and the Eloquence-2003/emulation example.

Note: The article names Kitten TTS, Supertonic, and Kokoro specifically but provides no quantified head-to-head metrics (STOI/intelligibility scores) between engines — its evidence is qualitative practitioner observation plus the dependency-count figures (Kitten TTS ~103 packages, Supertonic ~30).
